key_insight: The paper reframes PINN–FEM coupling as a Steklov–Poincaré interface problem and shows that Robin–Neumann coupling stabilizes the exchange and contracts even when the PINN acts as a perturbed operator.
engineering_relevance: This enables meshless fluid-structure/contact simulations that can handle moving interfaces and topology change without remeshing, while retaining the robustness of FEM on the other domain.
method: Model each solver as a trace-to-flux (Steklov–Poincaré) operator across a shared interface.
Replace unstable Dirichlet–Neumann coupling with Robin–Neumann coupling using a closed-form interface impedance.
Use a Fourier-mode interface probe to estimate the PINN’s resolvable interface spectrum and diagnose spectral limits.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports agreement with measured PINN-FEM contraction rates within 7% on 1D/2D Poisson tests, 0.5% accuracy for recovered interface eigenvalues, and 0.4% error in the submerged-weight contact reaction on a Stokes/rigid-disc benchmark.
watch_item: The approach still depends on PINN training quality; long-horizon drift off the Stokes manifold and under-resolved pre-impact squeeze-film effects remain concerns.
paper_title: Robin-Neumann Coupling of PINN and FEM Solvers: A Steklov-Poincaré View, with Application to Fluid-Structure Interaction with Contact
